one_line_takeaway: A PRISMA systematic review that codes 192 LLM-ethics papers into 8 major themes and argues hallucination, citation-integrity accountability, and censorship are LLM-unique ethical challenges; it is an ethics roadmap/survey, not a security contribution, and its eye-catching numbers all come from cited works.
key_finding: The paper's own output is the categorization itself, not new measurements. It reports how the 192-paper corpus distributes across its major codes (per Table 2, counted by number of reviewed papers): Case Studies 25, Mitigation Strategies 26, Bias & Fairness 23, Accountability & Governance 22, Misinformation & Disinformation 13, Ethical Concerns 14, Ethical Frameworks & Platforms 12, Privacy & Data Security 10, Transparency 7, Abusive LLM/hate speech/cyber-bullying 7, Auditing 7, Intellectual Property & Plagiarism 6, Censorship 1. Its analytical claim is that hallucination, verifiable accountability/citation integrity, and censorship-decoding are ethical challenges UNIQUE to LLMs versus traditional AI, motivating domain-specific frameworks and dynamic auditing. NOTE: numeric performance figures appearing in the text (e.g., a hallucination-detection method cutting GPT-3.5 hallucinations from 47.5% to 14.5%; the FaiRLLM benchmark; OccuQuest with 1000+ occupations across 26 categories; StereoSet/CrowS-Pairs) are results reported by CITED third-party works summarized in the survey, NOT experiments run by these authors.
core_contribution: A PRISMA-based systematic literature review of LLM ethics. Its own contribution is a synthesized taxonomy of the field derived from qualitative coding of 192 selected papers: four researchers distilled the corpus into major ethical 'key codes' and 13 findings subsections (the prose states 'eight major key codes' but Table 2 actually enumerates 13 major-code rows, an internal inconsistency in the paper itself) (ethical concerns; ethical frameworks/platforms; bias & fairness with sub-themes for bias types, fairness, and detection methods; privacy & data security; misinformation & disinformation; accountability & governance; case studies; mitigation strategies; transparency; censorship; intellectual property & plagiarism; abusive content/hate speech/cyberbullying; auditing). It then adds a Discussion (11 subsections) arguing for LLM-specific ethical concerns (hallucination, verifiable accountability/citation-integrity systems, decoding censorship complexity) that it claims are distinct from traditional AI ethics, and recommends domain-tailored ethical frameworks and dynamic/continuous auditing tools plus interdisciplinary collaboration. It is a survey/roadmap, not a new method, system, or empirical study.
threat_model: N/A. This is a systematic ethics review, not a security paper; it does not define an adversary, attack surface, or defense. It surveys privacy/data-security concerns (e.g., training-data memorization and extraction attacks, backdoors in word embeddings) as ONE of many ethical dimensions covered, always by citing other authors' work rather than proposing or evaluating a threat model of its own. No threat model is invented here to avoid forcing a security frame onto a non-security paper.
limitations: As a survey it produces no new empirical evaluation, benchmark, or artifact; all quantitative results are secondhand from cited papers. Search is restricted to English-language work and Google Scholar with an author-chosen page cutoff per keyword, and the 456-of-1147 relevance filtering plus 192-paper selection rest on subjective 'dominant topic' judgments, so coverage/selection bias is likely. Coding into 8 codes / 13 themes is qualitative and researcher-defined (four coders) with no reported inter-rater reliability. Some category counts are very thin (Censorship = 1 paper), limiting the strength of conclusions drawn there. The claim that certain challenges are 'unique' to LLMs is argued, not empirically demonstrated. The PDF also cites very recent models (e.g., DeepSeek, Grok 3) suggesting later revision beyond the 2024 arXiv date, but the review's evidentiary base is the fixed 192-paper corpus.

datasets_used: Corpus of 192 peer-reviewed papers selected for the systematic review (456 judged relevant out of 1147 search-result appearances; 17 found via manual search; 264 excluded)
No experimental datasets of the authors' own; benchmarks/datasets named (FaiRLLM, StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs, XNLI, FLORES, OccuQuest) are attributes of cited third-party studies, not used by this paper
quantitative_results: OWN review counts: 192 papers included, 8 major key codes, 13 findings subsections, 11 discussion subsections

OWN per-code paper counts (Table 2): Mitigation 26, Case Studies 25, Bias & Fairness 23, Accountability & Governance 22, Ethical Concerns 14, Misinformation 13, Frameworks 12, Privacy & Data Security 10, Transparency 7, Abusive/hate speech 7, Auditing 7, IP & Plagiarism 6, Censorship 1
CITED (not the authors' own): a hallucination detect-and-mitigate method reduced GPT-3.5 hallucinations from ~47.5% to 14.5% [ref 143]
CITED (not the authors' own): OccuQuest instruction-tuning dataset spans 1000+ occupations across 26 occupational categories [ref 132/133]
baselines_compared: None of the authors' own — this is a survey with no experimental baseline comparison

Baseline comparisons mentioned are internal to cited works (e.g., knowledge-unlearning vs. prior data-preprocessing/decoding privacy methods [135]; Fine-mixing/E-PUR vs. conventional backdoor-mitigation methods [134]; UP5 vs. fairness-aware recommendation baselines [55])
